content.text:
Design a Real-Time Driver Heatmap System
HARD
role
Software Development Engineer 2
Round 4
System Design HLD
System Design (HLD)
exp
#1662 · Apr 2026
○ no offer
High-Level Design (HLD)
System Design
Geo-spatial Algorithms
Real-Time Systems
Scalability
analytics/dashboard
Geohashing

▸
View problem statement & approach
Problem statement
Design a high-scale system to visualize the real-time density of drivers (a heatmap) across a city. The system must process frequent location updates from millions of drivers and provide aggregated views at varying map precision levels (e.g., using geohashing).

Requirements:

Handle high-frequency location ingestion (latitude, longitude, timestamp).
Efficiently aggregate data per minute/area for real-time visualization.
Support low-latency queries for heatmap rendering at different zoom levels.
Propose strategies for handling high update volumes and ensuring data persistence for historical analysis.

input.content:
drivers = 5_000_000
# update every 4 seconds
interval = 4
writes = drivers/interval
print("avg location updates/s:", writes)
print("peak (2x):", writes*2)

# bytes per update
b = 8+8+8+8  # driverid, lat, lon, ts
print("ingest bandwidth MB/s avg:", writes*b/1e6)

# heatmap queries: dashboards + rider apps
# say 1M concurrent map viewers refreshing every 10s
viewers = 1_000_000
qps = viewers/10
print("heatmap query qps:", qps)

# geohash cells precision 6 ~ 1.2km x 0.6km. city
# aggregated state: number of cells per city precision7 ~150m
# storage per-minute snapshots historical
cells_per_city = 200_000
minutes_day = 1440
bytes_per_cell = 30
days = 365
raw = cells_per_city*minutes_day*bytes_per_cell*days
print("historical agg/year GB (1 city):", raw/1e9)

# raw ingest if persisted
raw_ingest_day = writes*86400*b
print("raw ingest/day TB:", raw_ingest_day/1e12)
print("raw ingest/year PB:", raw_ingest_day*365/1e15)

print("historical agg/year GB (1 city):", raw/1e9)
raw_ingest_day = writes*86400*b

print("raw ingest/year PB:", raw_ingest_day*365/1e15)

content.text:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional) > 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the ingestion firehose, the geo-aggregation, and multi-zoom query serving — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?" The core tension: **millions of writes per second pouring in, but the read side wants a cheap, already-aggregated picture at many zoom levels.** This is fundamentally a *write-heavy stream that we pre-aggregate so reads are trivial* — we never query raw points to render a heatmap. 🏢 I'll target an **Uber/DoorDash-style bar** — real-time, geo-first, state-machine and hot-city-shard thinking. Tell me if it's a different company and I'll re-tilt. **Functional (above the line — priority order):** - **F1 — Ingest high-frequency location updates.** Drivers should be able to push (lat, lon, timestamp) every few seconds without us dropping data. - **F2 — Aggregate density per area per minute.** The system should roll raw points up into per-geohash-cell driver counts on a ~1-minute cadence. - **F3 — Serve low-latency heatmap queries at multiple zoom levels.** A viewer should be able to fetch the density grid for a viewport at a chosen precision in tens of milliseconds. - **F4 — Persist aggregates for historical analysis.** We should keep per-minute cell aggregates for later trend/OLAP queries. **Below the line (out of scope):** driver-rider matching/dispatch, exact per-driver tracking/replay, surge *pricing* logic (we feed it, don't compute it), ETA, auth provisioning of drivers. **Non-Functional:** - **Availability:** heatmap read path 99.9% (it's a visualization — brief staleness is fine); ingest 99.99% (losing the firehose is worse than a stale map). - **Latency:** heatmap query p99 < 100 ms; ingest is fire-and-forget, p99 ack < 50 ms. - **Freshness:** heatmap reflects reality within ~1 minute (this is the real SLO, not strong consistency). - **Consistency:** **eventual / approximate is acceptable everywhere** — a count of "47 vs 49 drivers in a cell" is irrelevant to a heatmap. This is the key relaxation that unlocks the whole design. - **Scale:** millions of active drivers, ~1M+ concurrent map viewers, global but city-partitioned. > 💬 "The thing I want to flag early: a heatmap doesn't need exact counts or strong consistency. That single relaxation is what lets me trade precision for massive throughput." ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** exact read-your-own-writes is *not* a goal here — a driver won't see their own dot pop instantly, and that's intentional. I'll revisit in the ledger. > 🎙️ Script: "So we're building a real-time density heatmap of drivers across cities. Four things matter: ingest a firehose of location pings, aggregate them per area per minute, serve that grid fast at any zoom, and keep history for analytics. The big insight driving everything is that a heatmap is approximate by nature — nobody cares if a cell says 47 or 49 — so I'll treat the whole thing as eventually consistent and spend my engineering budget on write throughput and read latency, not on exactness." ## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions **Questions I'd ask (and why each changes the design):** - **How many active drivers and what ping frequency?** → sets the write QPS; the difference between 200K/s and 2M/s decides whether we need a stream layer at all. - **How many concurrent viewers and refresh rate?** → sizes the read tier and whether we can serve from a single cache. - **What zoom/precision levels?** → decides how many geohash precision tiers we pre-aggregate vs compute on the fly. - **How fresh must the map be — seconds or a minute?** → a 1-min window lets us batch cheaply; sub-second forces a streaming aggregator. - **How long must history be kept and for whom (analytics/ML/compliance)?** → sizes cold storage and whether we keep raw pings or only aggregates. - **Global or single-region?** → drives geo-routing and whether each city is independent. **Assumptions I'll proceed with:** - ~5M active drivers, ping every ~4s. - ~1M concurrent viewers refreshing every ~10s. - Geohash precisions 4–7 (city → block) cover the zoom range. - Freshness target ~1 minute; map can lag slightly. - Keep **per-minute aggregates for 1+ year**; keep raw pings ~7–30 days in cheap storage for reprocessing. - Cities are independent partitions (a query is always scoped to a viewport/region). 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — most important is the ~1-minute freshness target and that approximate counts are fine. If you need sub-second or exact counts, tell me now because it changes the aggregation layer significantly." ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) I'm deferring full estimation — here are only the few numbers that actually drive design decisions. | Metric | Figure (rounded) | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Avg location updates | ~1.25M/s | Forces a streaming buffer, not direct DB writes | | Peak updates | ~2.5M/s | Sizes Kafka partitions + aggregator fleet | | Ingest bandwidth | ~40 MB/s avg | Cheap — payload is tiny, QPS is the problem | | Heatmap query QPS | ~100K/s | Must be served from cache, never raw data | | Aggregate history (1 city/yr) | ~3 TB | Cheap columnar storage | | Raw pings if persisted | ~3.5 TB/day, ~1.3 PB/yr | Why we keep raw only days, aggregates for years | **QPS** *(queries/writes per second — the request rate)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — auto-expiry on cached data so stale cells vanish)*. 🔑 **The one number that forces the design: ~2.5M location writes/s at peak.** A single database or Redis node tops out around ~100–150K ops/s, so we are **~20x over a single node** — we *cannot* write pings to a DB directly. The flip threshold: below ~100K/s I'd skip the stream layer and write straight to a sharded store. Above it, we **must** buffer in a partitioned log and aggregate before any durable write. > 🎙️ Script: "The headline is the asymmetry. We're taking about 1.25 million location updates a second, peaking near 2.5 million — that's twenty-plus times what any single node handles, so raw pings can never touch a database directly; they go into a partitioned log and get aggregated first. Meanwhile reads are only about a hundred thousand a second, and they all want the same pre-computed grid, so those come straight out of a cache in single-digit milliseconds. Storage is interesting too: raw pings would be over a petabyte a year, but the per-minute aggregates are only a few terabytes — so I keep raw for days and aggregates for years." ## 4. Core Entities First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later: - **Location Update** — a single driver ping: driverId, lat, lon, timestamp. Ephemeral. - **Geohash Cell** — an area at a given precision; the unit we aggregate into. - **Cell Aggregate** — count (and maybe avg/availability) of drivers in a cell for a 1-minute window. The thing we actually serve. - **Heatmap Tile / Snapshot** — the set of cell aggregates for a viewport+zoom at a point in time. - **Driver** — identity behind a ping (used only to dedup/last-seen, not stored long-term here). > 🎙️ Script: "The key nouns are the raw location update, the geohash cell we bucket it into, the per-minute cell aggregate that's the real product, and the heatmap tile a viewport assembles from those aggregates. I'll firm up the exact fields once we've drawn the flow." ## 5. API / Interface Going one-by-one through the functional requirements: **F1 — Ingest (driver → us):** ``` POST /v1/locations Body: { driverId, lat, lon, ts } Resp: 202 Accepted ``` Why POST + 202: it's a fire-and-forget create; we accept and buffer, we don't block on durable write. **Security:** driverId comes from the driver's JWT *(a signed token proving identity)* — never trust a client-supplied driverId. Updates are naturally idempotent on `(driverId, ts)` — a retried ping just overwrites the same bucket slot. **F3 — Read heatmap:** ``` GET /v1/heatmap?bbox=lat1,lon1,lat2,lon2&precision=6&t=latest Resp: 200 { cells: [ {geohash, count}, ... ], asOf } ``` Why GET: pure read, cacheable at CDN/edge for a few seconds. `precision` maps to zoom; `bbox` is the viewport. **F4 — Historical query (analyst):** ``` GET /v1/heatmap/history?geohash=tdr1&from=...&to=...&granularity=minute ``` Backed by the columnar store, not the live cache. > 💬 "Note the ingest endpoint just returns 202 — we acknowledge receipt and move on. The read endpoint is a plain cacheable GET so a CDN soaks up most of the 100K QPS." > 🎙️ Script: "Drivers POST their location and get an instant 202 — we never make them wait on a write. Viewers do a cacheable GET with a bounding box and a precision level, and get back a list of cells with counts. Analysts hit a separate history endpoint that goes to the columnar store. Identity always comes from the token, never the request body." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Ingest high-frequency location updates" Start minimal: drivers hit a stateless ingest service behind a load balancer, which writes each ping into a partitioned log. No database yet. ```mermaid flowchart TD D[Drivers] -->|1 POST location| LB[Load balancer] LB -->|2 route| IS[Ingest service] IS -->|3 produce keyed by geohash prefix| K[[Kafka log]] classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class IS,K cold; ``` 1. Driver POSTs a ping; LB spreads load across stateless ingest pods. 2. Ingest validates the JWT, derives the driver's geohash, and (3) **produces** the event to **Kafka** *(a durable, partitioned append-only log that absorbs a firehose so no ping is lost if downstream is slow — it replaces writing to a DB we can't keep up with)*. We partition by **geohash prefix** so all pings for a city land on the same partitions — co-locating the data the aggregator needs. > 💬 "I'm resisting a database here on purpose. At 2.5M writes a second nothing durable keeps up, so I buffer in Kafka and let consumers aggregate at their own pace." ### 6.2 "Aggregate density per area per minute" Now add a stream processor that consumes the log and maintains per-cell, per-minute counts. ```mermaid flowchart TD K[[Kafka log]] -->|4 consume| F[Stream aggregator Flink] F -->|5 windowed counts per geohash| RW[Redis live aggregates] F -->|6 sealed minute| OLAP[(Columnar store)] classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class F,RW,OLAP cold; ``` 4. **Flink** *(a stateful stream processor that runs tumbling 1-minute windows and counts events per key — it does the rollup so reads never touch raw data)* consumes each partition. 5. For each 1-minute tumbling window it emits `count per geohash` at the finest precision (p7) into **Redis** *(an in-memory key-value store — holds the current live grid for fast reads)*. 6. When a minute "seals," the aggregator also writes the finalized counts to the **columnar store** for history (F4). **Roll-up trick:** we only count at the finest precision (p7); coarser zoom levels (p6, p5, p4) are just **prefix sums** — a p6 cell's count is the sum of its child p7 cells. So one aggregation feeds every zoom level. > 💬 "Geohash has a lovely property: dropping a character zooms out one level. So I aggregate once at the finest grid and sum prefixes for coarser zooms — no separate pipeline per zoom level." ### 6.3 "Serve low-latency heatmap queries at multiple zoom levels" Add the read path: a query service that reads pre-aggregated cells from Redis, fronted by a CDN. ```mermaid flowchart TD V[Map viewers] -->|7 GET heatmap bbox precision| CDN[CDN edge cache] CDN -->|8 miss| QS[Query service] QS -->|9 read cells in bbox| RW[(Redis live aggregates)] RW -->|10 cells| QS --> CDN --> V classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class CDN,QS,RW hot; ``` 7. Viewer GETs the heatmap for a viewport+precision; (8) the **CDN** *(edge cache near the user)* serves most requests directly with a ~5–10s TTL. 9–10. On a miss, the query service computes which geohash cells fall in the bbox at that precision and does a single multi-key read from Redis, returning the grid. ### Final (high-level) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Write D[Drivers] -->|1 POST| IS[Ingest service] IS -->|2 produce| K[[Kafka by geohash]] K -->|3 consume| F[Flink 1-min windows] end subgraph ReadHot V[Viewers] -->|7 GET| CDN[CDN edge] CDN -->|miss| QS[Query service] QS --> RW[(Redis live grid
key geohash7 count)] end subgraph History OLAP[(Columnar store
minute,geohash,count)] S3[(Raw pings 7-30d)] end F -->|4 live counts| RW F -->|5 sealed minute| OLAP IS -.->|optional archive| S3 classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class CDN,QS,RW hot; class IS,K,F,OLAP,S3 cold; ``` > 🎙️ Script: "End to end: drivers POST pings, the ingest service shoves them into Kafka partitioned by city geohash, and Flink consumes those partitions running one-minute windows to count drivers per finest-grid cell. Those live counts go into Redis as the current grid, and each sealed minute also lands in a columnar store for history. On the read side, viewers hit a CDN that absorbs most traffic; misses go to a query service that just reads the cells in the viewport out of Redis. Coarser zoom levels are prefix sums of the fine grid, so one aggregation serves every zoom." 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton. The three places I can go deep are the ingestion firehose, the geo-aggregation and zoom roll-ups, or the hot-city scaling problem. Which is most interesting to you?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Fields | Store | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Location update | driverId, lat, lon, ts | Kafka (then S3 archive) | Append-only firehose; never queried by key | | Live cell aggregate | geohash7 → count, asOf | **Redis** (sharded by geohash prefix) | In-memory, ~100K read QPS, TTL auto-expires stale cells | | Historical aggregate | minute, geohash, precision, count | **Columnar OLAP** (ClickHouse/Druid) | Time-range scans, cheap compression | | Raw archive | partitioned parquet by hour/city | **S3** *(cheap object storage)* | Reprocessing / ML; deleted after 7–30d | - **Partition/shard key everywhere: geohash prefix.** Co-locates a city's data for both aggregation and reads, and a viewport query hits a bounded key range. - **Consistency:** Redis aggregates are eventually consistent with ~1-min lag; columnar store is the durable record of truth for history. - **TTL on Redis cells** (~2–3 min) so a cell that goes empty disappears instead of showing a stale ghost count. > 🎙️ Script: "Three stores for three jobs. Kafka and S3 hold the raw firehose nobody queries directly. Redis holds the live grid keyed by geohash for fast reads with a short TTL so empty cells vanish. And a columnar store like ClickHouse holds per-minute aggregates for years of cheap time-range analytics. Everything is partitioned by geohash prefix so a city's data stays together." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the firehose number (2.5M writes/s) and the 1-minute freshness SLO — every decision falls out of those two. ### How do we handle 2.5M location writes/s without melting a database? **#### Bad: write each ping straight to a geo-database** **Approach:** ingest service does an upsert per ping into Postgres/Mongo with a geo-index. **Challenges:** ~2.5M upserts/s is ~20x a single node; index maintenance per write is brutal; you'd shard into hundreds of nodes purely for write throughput you don't even need at read time. ⚠️ **Trap:** treating location pings as durable records — they're disposable; only the *aggregate* matters. **#### Good: batch into Redis counters directly** **Approach:** ingest increments a Redis counter per geohash cell. **Challenges:** still ~2.5M INCRs/s hammering hot city keys; no replay if Redis dies; counts drift because pings don't decrement when a driver leaves a cell — you'd need TTL gymnastics per driver. **#### Great: Kafka buffer + Flink windowed aggregation** **Approach:** pings → Kafka (partitioned by geohash prefix) → Flink tumbling 1-minute windows counting **distinct drivers per cell** → write the finished count to Redis. The window naturally handles "driver left the cell": each minute we recount from scratch, so counts self-heal — no decrement bookkeeping. ```mermaid flowchart TD K[[Kafka geohash partitions]] -->|consume| F[Flink tumbling 1-min] F -->|distinct driver count per geohash7| RW[(Redis grid)] F -->|sealed minute| OLAP[(Columnar store)] classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class F,RW,OLAP cold; ``` **Math:** 2.5M/s spread over ~250 Kafka partitions = ~10K msgs/s/partition — trivial. Flink parallelism scales with partitions. Redis only sees **one write per active cell per minute** — at ~200K active cells/city that's a few thousand writes/s, *four orders of magnitude* below the raw firehose. That collapse is the whole point. **Trade-off:** ~1-minute latency floor and Flink operational complexity — acceptable given the freshness SLO. > 🎙️ Script: "The trick is to never write pings durably. They go into Kafka, partitioned by city, and Flink counts distinct drivers per cell in one-minute windows. Because we recount each minute, a driver leaving a cell is handled for free — the count just doesn't include them next window. Redis then only gets one write per cell per minute, which turns a 2.5-million-a-second firehose into a few thousand writes a second." 🧠 If they ask "why not just sample pings?": "We can — dropping to 1 ping per driver per window cuts volume with zero accuracy loss for counts, since the window only needs to know a driver was present once. Kafka+Flink still gives us replay and history that pure sampling doesn't." ### How do we serve every zoom level without N pipelines? **#### Great: aggregate at finest precision, prefix-sum for coarser zooms** **Approach:** Geohash is hierarchical — `tdr1y` (p5) contains all `tdr1y*` (p6) cells. We count once at p7 and **roll up by truncating the geohash string**. A query at precision 5 truncates each p7 key to 5 chars and sums. **Trade-off:** summing millions of fine cells at query time for a zoomed-out city view is expensive. So we **pre-materialize** the common coarse levels (p4–p6) in Flink too — emit counts at p4,5,6,7 from the same window. Storage is tiny (coarser = far fewer cells), reads become a direct key lookup at any zoom. ```mermaid flowchart TD F[Flink window] -->|p7 fine count| RW[(Redis)] F -->|p6 p5 p4 rolled up| RW QS[Query service] -->|read precision N keys in bbox| RW classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class QS,RW hot; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class F cold; ``` > 🎙️ Script: "Geohash is hierarchical, so zooming out is literally chopping characters off the key. I pre-compute the counts at four precision levels in the same Flink window — coarse levels are tiny — so any zoom is a direct Redis read, no on-the-fly summing." 🧠 If they ask "why geohash over S2 or H3?": "All work; geohash wins here because its string-prefix property makes zoom roll-ups trivial. H3's hexagons avoid distortion near poles and look nicer, so for a polished product I'd consider H3 with its parent-cell API — same idea, hex instead of prefix." ### Hot-city / hot-cell problem (the binding bottleneck) **The bottleneck:** key distribution is uniform but **traffic isn't** — downtown Manhattan at rush hour has 100x the drivers and viewers of a suburb. One Redis shard owning that geohash prefix and one Kafka partition become hot. **Relief:** - **Kafka:** sub-partition hot prefixes by appending a salt (`geohash7 + driverId%N`) so a hot cell spreads across N partitions; Flink re-keys and merges. New ceiling: per-partition load bounded regardless of city density. - **Redis reads:** the CDN already absorbs ~95% of reads for popular downtown viewports (everyone looks at the same area). Residual misses hit a read replica per shard. - **Hot shard:** replicate the hot city's shard 3–5x for reads. **Math:** 100K read QPS at 95% CDN hit = ~5K QPS to Redis — one replica handles it. Cold cache (0% hit): 100K QPS needs ~2–3 replicas per shard. Manageable because the working set (visible viewports) is small. > 🎙️ Script: "The real danger isn't average load, it's downtown at rush hour. I salt hot geohash prefixes across multiple Kafka partitions so no single one melts, and on reads the CDN saves me — everyone's looking at the same downtown tile, so cache hit rates are high and Redis barely feels it." 🧠 If they ask "what if a single cell has a million drivers?": "Counts are cheap regardless of cardinality if I use a counter; if I want distinct-driver counts I'd use HyperLogLog *(a probabilistic counter that estimates uniques in ~12KB with ~2% error)* — perfect since heatmaps are approximate anyway." ### Idempotency & duplicate pings A driver's retry or at-least-once Kafka delivery can double-count. **Within a 1-minute window we count distinct driverId**, so a duplicate ping is naturally deduped — the same driver in the same cell counts once regardless of how many pings arrive. This makes the whole pipeline idempotent without a separate dedup store. A driver straddling two cells in one minute may be counted in both; for a heatmap that's acceptable noise. > 🎙️ Script: "Idempotency falls out of counting distinct drivers per window — duplicate pings collapse to one. I don't need a dedup store; the aggregation is dedup." ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost **Availability:** ingest 99.99% (Kafka replicated 3x, multi-AZ), read 99.9%. **Per-dependency degradation:** - **Redis down:** query service falls back to reading the *last sealed minute* from the columnar store — slower but the map still renders. Stale-but-up. - **Flink down:** Kafka retains data (replay on recovery); map freezes at last-good minute, no data lost. This is why the buffer matters. - **Kafka partition loss:** 3x replication + multi-AZ; consumers resume from committed offset. - **CDN down:** query service + replicas absorb full 100K QPS at higher cost. **RPO/RTO:** Raw pings RPO = a few seconds (Kafka), but they're disposable. Aggregates RPO ~1 min (last sealed window in OLAP), RTO ~minutes (Flink restart from offset). History (OLAP) is the durable record — replicated, backed up; tested restore monthly. **Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is **Kafka + Flink compute** for the firehose (~2.5M/s sustained) and CDN egress for reads, not storage — aggregates are only a few TB. Raw archive in S3 is cheap because we expire it in days. Ballpark mid five figures/month for a large multi-city deployment; I'd cut it by sampling pings (1 per driver per window) — the single biggest lever. **Observability:** SLO burn-rate alerts on (a) freshness lag = now − last sealed minute, (b) Kafka consumer lag, (c) read p99; distributed tracing on the read path; runbook for "map is stale" → check consumer lag first. > 🎙️ Script: "The buffer is also my reliability story. If Flink or Redis dies, Kafka holds everything and the map just freezes at the last good minute instead of losing data — and if Redis is gone, reads fall back to the last sealed minute in the columnar store. Nothing here is a single point of total failure, and the dominant cost is the streaming compute, which I'd attack by sampling pings." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger - **1-minute aggregation latency (least sure):** gives huge throughput collapse and self-healing counts, but the map lags reality by up to a minute. If the SLO became sub-second, I'd switch to sliding windows + incremental counters and pay in complexity and cost. - **Approximate counts / sampling:** we accept noise to save compute. Reverses if a use case (e.g. driver pay, compliance) needs exact counts — then we drop sampling and add exactly-once Flink checkpointing. - **Geohash over H3:** prefix roll-ups are simplest, but rectangular cells distort and look blocky. At product-polish scale I'd move to H3 hexagons; reverses on UX priority, not throughput. - **Pre-materializing 4 zoom levels:** trades a little write/storage for O(1) reads at any zoom. Reverses only if we needed dozens of arbitrary precision levels — then compute-on-read with caching. > 🎙️ Script: "My least-certain call is the one-minute window — it's the source of all the efficiency but also the staleness. If they need real-time-real-time, that's the first thing I'd revisit, trading simplicity for sliding windows." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **Q1. Why Kafka and not just a load balancer to aggregators?** Kafka gives durable buffering, replay, and decoupling: if aggregators fall behind or crash, no pings are lost and they resume from offset. A direct LB-to-aggregator path drops data the moment a consumer hiccups. It also lets us partition by geohash so each city's data is co-located. The cost is operational complexity, which is worth it at 2.5M/s. > 💬 "Kafka is my shock absorber — it decouples a firehose I can't slow down from consumers that occasionally stall." **Q2. How do counts decrease when drivers leave an area?** We don't decrement — each 1-minute tumbling window recounts from scratch, so a driver who left simply isn't present in the next window's count. This self-healing avoids fragile per-driver decrement bookkeeping. The cost is up to a minute of staleness, which matches our SLO. > 💬 "I never decrement. Every minute I recount, so leaving a cell is handled for free." **Q3. How do you serve different zoom levels efficiently?** Geohash is hierarchical, so I aggregate at the finest precision and pre-materialize coarser levels by truncating the key in the same Flink window. Any zoom becomes a direct Redis lookup of the right-precision keys in the viewport. No query-time summation of millions of cells. > 💬 "Zooming out is just chopping characters off a geohash, and I pre-compute those levels so every zoom is a direct read." **Q4. What happens at peak in a dense downtown — hot shards?** That's the binding bottleneck: traffic is non-uniform even if keys are. I salt hot geohash prefixes across multiple Kafka partitions and re-merge in Flink, and on reads the CDN absorbs most of the duplicate downtown viewport requests. The hot city's Redis shard gets extra read replicas. > 💬 "Downtown rush hour is the real test — I salt hot keys across partitions and lean on the CDN since everyone's viewing the same tile." **Q5. How do you avoid double-counting from retries / at-least-once delivery?** The window counts distinct driverIds, so duplicate pings collapse to one — the aggregation is inherently idempotent. No separate dedup store needed. A driver straddling a cell boundary may appear in two cells, which is acceptable heatmap noise. > 💬 "Counting distinct drivers per window means duplicates just disappear — dedup is free." **Q6. How is history stored and queried?** Each sealed minute's aggregates go to a columnar store like ClickHouse, partitioned by time and geohash, giving cheap compressed time-range scans for trend analysis. Raw pings go to S3 as parquet but expire in days since they're a petabyte/year. Analysts query the OLAP store, never the live cache. > 💬 "Per-minute aggregates live for years in a columnar store; raw pings live for days in S3 for reprocessing." **Q7. Exactly-once vs at-least-once?** Default is at-least-once with idempotent distinct-counting, which is cheaper and sufficient for approximate heatmaps. If a downstream consumer needed exactness (billing, compliance), I'd enable Flink's checkpointed exactly-once with transactional Kafka writes, paying in latency and throughput. I'd scope exactly-once only to that consumer. > 💬 "At-least-once is fine because the count is idempotent; I'd only pay for exactly-once where money depends on it." **Q8. How fresh is the map, really, and can you make it faster?** Up to ~1 minute behind, set by the tumbling window. To get to seconds I'd use overlapping sliding windows or incremental counters that emit every few seconds, at higher compute cost and more complex state. I'd only do it if a product need justified it. > 💬 "It's about a minute fresh; sub-second is possible with sliding windows but I wouldn't pay for it unless asked." **Q9. How do you cap the read load — 100K QPS is a lot?** Most viewers look at the same popular viewports, so a CDN with a 5–10s TTL gives ~95% hit rates, leaving only a few thousand QPS to Redis replicas. The TTL also bounds staleness to the same order as our freshness SLO, so caching costs us nothing in correctness. Cold-cache worst case needs 2–3 replicas per shard. > 💬 "The CDN does the heavy lifting — everyone looks at downtown, so hit rates are high and Redis barely sweats." **Q10. How would you extend this to show driver *availability* heat, not just density?** Each ping carries a status (available/on-trip); Flink keys the window by (geohash, status) and emits separate counts, or maintains a ratio per cell. Redis stores both layers, and the read API takes a `layer` param. Same pipeline, richer payload — no architectural change, which is the point of aggregating generically. > 💬 "I'd just add status to the window key and emit per-status counts — the pipeline doesn't change, the payload gets richer." **Q11. What if you need this globally, multi-region?** Cities are independent, so I run the pipeline per region and route by geohash — a viewport query is always region-local, so no cross-region reads on the hot path. History replicates async to a central OLAP for global analytics. Single-write-region per city avoids multi-master conflicts entirely. > 💬 "Geo-partitioning makes multi-region easy — each city is its own pipeline and queries are always local." **Q12. How do you handle bad/spoofed GPS data?** Validate lat/lon ranges and plausibility (speed jumps) at ingest, and since counts are approximate, a few bad points barely move a cell. For systematic spoofing I'd add anomaly detection on driver trajectories downstream, but it's out of the heatmap's critical path. The aggregation's averaging nature is itself robust to noise. > 💬 "Heatmaps are averages, so noise washes out; I do basic plausibility checks at ingest and leave fraud detection to a separate consumer." 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "We're building a real-time driver density heatmap. The defining fact is asymmetry — about two and a half million location pings a second coming in, versus a hundred thousand reads that all want the same pre-computed grid. So pings never touch a database: they go into Kafka partitioned by city geohash, and Flink counts distinct drivers per cell in one-minute windows. That collapses the firehose into a few thousand Redis writes a second, and because we recount each minute, drivers leaving a cell self-heal with no bookkeeping. We aggregate at the finest geohash precision and pre-materialize coarser zoom levels by truncating the key, so any zoom is a direct lookup. Reads go through a CDN that absorbs most traffic since everyone views the same downtown tiles. Sealed minutes land in a columnar store for years of cheap history; raw pings expire in days. The whole thing is intentionally approximate and eventually consistent — that single relaxation is what makes the scale tractable. The real risks I'd watch are hot downtown shards, which I handle with key-salting and CDN caching, and the one-minute freshness floor, which I'd only tighten with sliding windows if the product truly needed it."

Design a Real-Time Driver Heatmap System
HARD
role
Software Development Engineer 2

System Design (HLD)
exp
#1662 · Apr 2026
○ no offer
High-Level Design (HLD)
System Design
Geo-spatial Algorithms
Real-Time Systems
Scalability
analytics/dashboard
Geohashing

▸
View problem statement & approach
Problem statement
Design a high-scale system to visualize the real-time density of drivers (a heatmap) across a city. The system must process frequent location updates from millions of drivers and provide aggregated views at varying map precision levels (e.g., using geohashing).

Requirements:

Handle high-frequency location ingestion (latitude, longitude, timestamp).
Efficiently aggregate data per minute/area for real-time visualization.
Support low-latency queries for heatmap rendering at different zoom levels.
Propose strategies for handling high update volumes and ensuring data persistence for historical analysis.

transcript.thinking: I need to figure out the best approach here—I could stream the opener separately, but since I can only produce one message, I'll compute the capacity numbers with a tool call and then present everything together with the opening text first. I don't have any current rewritten thinking to build upon, so I'm ready to begin. Once you provide the next thinking to rewrite, I'll compress it into 1-3 sentences of natural first-person prose, describing any code conceptually rather than reproducing it verbatim.

blocks.name: run_python

input.content:
drivers = 5_000_000
# update every 4 seconds
interval = 4
writes = drivers/interval
print("avg location updates/s:", writes)
print("peak (2x):", writes*2)

# bytes per update
b = 8+8+8+8  # driverid, lat, lon, ts
print("ingest bandwidth MB/s avg:", writes*b/1e6)

# heatmap queries: dashboards + rider apps
# say 1M concurrent map viewers refreshing every 10s
viewers = 1_000_000
qps = viewers/10
print("heatmap query qps:", qps)

# geohash cells precision 6 ~ 1.2km x 0.6km. city
# aggregated state: number of cells per city precision7 ~150m
# storage per-minute snapshots historical
cells_per_city = 200_000
minutes_day = 1440
bytes_per_cell = 30
days = 365
raw = cells_per_city*minutes_day*bytes_per_cell*days
print("historical agg/year GB (1 city):", raw/1e9)

# raw ingest if persisted
raw_ingest_day = writes*86400*b
print("raw ingest/day TB:", raw_ingest_day/1e12)
print("raw ingest/year PB:", raw_ingest_day*365/1e15)

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional) > 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the ingestion firehose, the geo-aggregation, and multi-zoom query serving — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?" The core tension: **millions of writes per second pouring in, but the read side wants a cheap, already-aggregated picture at many zoom levels.** This is fundamentally a *write-heavy stream that we pre-aggregate so reads are trivial* — we never query raw points to render a heatmap. 🏢 I'll target an **Uber/DoorDash-style bar** — real-time, geo-first, state-machine and hot-city-shard thinking. Tell me if it's a different company and I'll re-tilt. **Functional (above the line — priority order):** - **F1 — Ingest high-frequency location updates.** Drivers should be able to push (lat, lon, timestamp) every few seconds without us dropping data. - **F2 — Aggregate density per area per minute.** The system should roll raw points up into per-geohash-cell driver counts on a ~1-minute cadence. - **F3 — Serve low-latency heatmap queries at multiple zoom levels.** A viewer should be able to fetch the density grid for a viewport at a chosen precision in tens of milliseconds. - **F4 — Persist aggregates for historical analysis.** We should keep per-minute cell aggregates for later trend/OLAP queries. **Below the line (out of scope):** driver-rider matching/dispatch, exact per-driver tracking/replay, surge *pricing* logic (we feed it, don't compute it), ETA, auth provisioning of drivers. **Non-Functional:** - **Availability:** heatmap read path 99.9% (it's a visualization — brief staleness is fine); ingest 99.99% (losing the firehose is worse than a stale map). - **Latency:** heatmap query p99 < 100 ms; ingest is fire-and-forget, p99 ack < 50 ms. - **Freshness:** heatmap reflects reality within ~1 minute (this is the real SLO, not strong consistency). - **Consistency:** **eventual / approximate is acceptable everywhere** — a count of "47 vs 49 drivers in a cell" is irrelevant to a heatmap. This is the key relaxation that unlocks the whole design. - **Scale:** millions of active drivers, ~1M+ concurrent map viewers, global but city-partitioned. > 💬 "The thing I want to flag early: a heatmap doesn't need exact counts or strong consistency. That single relaxation is what lets me trade precision for massive throughput." ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** exact read-your-own-writes is *not* a goal here — a driver won't see their own dot pop instantly, and that's intentional. I'll revisit in the ledger. > 🎙️ Script: "So we're building a real-time density heatmap of drivers across cities. Four things matter: ingest a firehose of location pings, aggregate them per area per minute, serve that grid fast at any zoom, and keep history for analytics. The big insight driving everything is that a heatmap is approximate by nature — nobody cares if a cell says 47 or 49 — so I'll treat the whole thing as eventually consistent and spend my engineering budget on write throughput and read latency, not on exactness." ## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions **Questions I'd ask (and why each changes the design):** - **How many active drivers and what ping frequency?** → sets the write QPS; the difference between 200K/s and 2M/s decides whether we need a stream layer at all. - **How many concurrent viewers and refresh rate?** → sizes the read tier and whether we can serve from a single cache. - **What zoom/precision levels?** → decides how many geohash precision tiers we pre-aggregate vs compute on the fly. - **How fresh must the map be — seconds or a minute?** → a 1-min window lets us batch cheaply; sub-second forces a streaming aggregator. - **How long must history be kept and for whom (analytics/ML/compliance)?** → sizes cold storage and whether we keep raw pings or only aggregates. - **Global or single-region?** → drives geo-routing and whether each city is independent. **Assumptions I'll proceed with:** - ~5M active drivers, ping every ~4s. - ~1M concurrent viewers refreshing every ~10s. - Geohash precisions 4–7 (city → block) cover the zoom range. - Freshness target ~1 minute; map can lag slightly. - Keep **per-minute aggregates for 1+ year**; keep raw pings ~7–30 days in cheap storage for reprocessing. - Cities are independent partitions (a query is always scoped to a viewport/region). 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — most important is the ~1-minute freshness target and that approximate counts are fine. If you need sub-second or exact counts, tell me now because it changes the aggregation layer significantly." ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) I'm deferring full estimation — here are only the few numbers that actually drive design decisions. | Metric | Figure (rounded) | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Avg location updates | ~1.25M/s | Forces a streaming buffer, not direct DB writes | | Peak updates | ~2.5M/s | Sizes Kafka partitions + aggregator fleet | | Ingest bandwidth | ~40 MB/s avg | Cheap — payload is tiny, QPS is the problem | | Heatmap query QPS | ~100K/s | Must be served from cache, never raw data | | Aggregate history (1 city/yr) | ~3 TB | Cheap columnar storage | | Raw pings if persisted | ~3.5 TB/day, ~1.3 PB/yr | Why we keep raw only days, aggregates for years | **QPS** *(queries/writes per second — the request rate)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — auto-expiry on cached data so stale cells vanish)*. 🔑 **The one number that forces the design: ~2.5M location writes/s at peak.** A single database or Redis node tops out around ~100–150K ops/s, so we are **~20x over a single node** — we *cannot* write pings to a DB directly. The flip threshold: below ~100K/s I'd skip the stream layer and write straight to a sharded store. Above it, we **must** buffer in a partitioned log and aggregate before any durable write. > 🎙️ Script: "The headline is the asymmetry. We're taking about 1.25 million location updates a second, peaking near 2.5 million — that's twenty-plus times what any single node handles, so raw pings can never touch a database directly; they go into a partitioned log and get aggregated first. Meanwhile reads are only about a hundred thousand a second, and they all want the same pre-computed grid, so those come straight out of a cache in single-digit milliseconds. Storage is interesting too: raw pings would be over a petabyte a year, but the per-minute aggregates are only a few terabytes — so I keep raw for days and aggregates for years." ## 4. Core Entities First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later: - **Location Update** — a single driver ping: driverId, lat, lon, timestamp. Ephemeral. - **Geohash Cell** — an area at a given precision; the unit we aggregate into. - **Cell Aggregate** — count (and maybe avg/availability) of drivers in a cell for a 1-minute window. The thing we actually serve. - **Heatmap Tile / Snapshot** — the set of cell aggregates for a viewport+zoom at a point in time. - **Driver** — identity behind a ping (used only to dedup/last-seen, not stored long-term here). > 🎙️ Script: "The key nouns are the raw location update, the geohash cell we bucket it into, the per-minute cell aggregate that's the real product, and the heatmap tile a viewport assembles from those aggregates. I'll firm up the exact fields once we've drawn the flow." ## 5. API / Interface Going one-by-one through the functional requirements: **F1 — Ingest (driver → us):** ``` POST /v1/locations Body: { driverId, lat, lon, ts } Resp: 202 Accepted ``` Why POST + 202: it's a fire-and-forget create; we accept and buffer, we don't block on durable write. **Security:** driverId comes from the driver's JWT *(a signed token proving identity)* — never trust a client-supplied driverId. Updates are naturally idempotent on `(driverId, ts)` — a retried ping just overwrites the same bucket slot. **F3 — Read heatmap:** ``` GET /v1/heatmap?bbox=lat1,lon1,lat2,lon2&precision=6&t=latest Resp: 200 { cells: [ {geohash, count}, ... ], asOf } ``` Why GET: pure read, cacheable at CDN/edge for a few seconds. `precision` maps to zoom; `bbox` is the viewport. **F4 — Historical query (analyst):** ``` GET /v1/heatmap/history?geohash=tdr1&from=...&to=...&granularity=minute ``` Backed by the columnar store, not the live cache. > 💬 "Note the ingest endpoint just returns 202 — we acknowledge receipt and move on. The read endpoint is a plain cacheable GET so a CDN soaks up most of the 100K QPS." > 🎙️ Script: "Drivers POST their location and get an instant 202 — we never make them wait on a write. Viewers do a cacheable GET with a bounding box and a precision level, and get back a list of cells with counts. Analysts hit a separate history endpoint that goes to the columnar store. Identity always comes from the token, never the request body." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Ingest high-frequency location updates" Start minimal: drivers hit a stateless ingest service behind a load balancer, which writes each ping into a partitioned log. No database yet. ```mermaid flowchart TD D[Drivers] -->|1 POST location| LB[Load balancer] LB -->|2 route| IS[Ingest service] IS -->|3 produce keyed by geohash prefix| K[[Kafka log]] classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class IS,K cold; ``` 1. Driver POSTs a ping; LB spreads load across stateless ingest pods. 2. Ingest validates the JWT, derives the driver's geohash, and (3) **produces** the event to **Kafka** *(a durable, partitioned append-only log that absorbs a firehose so no ping is lost if downstream is slow — it replaces writing to a DB we can't keep up with)*. We partition by **geohash prefix** so all pings for a city land on the same partitions — co-locating the data the aggregator needs. > 💬 "I'm resisting a database here on purpose. At 2.5M writes a second nothing durable keeps up, so I buffer in Kafka and let consumers aggregate at their own pace." ### 6.2 "Aggregate density per area per minute" Now add a stream processor that consumes the log and maintains per-cell, per-minute counts. ```mermaid flowchart TD K[[Kafka log]] -->|4 consume| F[Stream aggregator Flink] F -->|5 windowed counts per geohash| RW[Redis live aggregates] F -->|6 sealed minute| OLAP[(Columnar store)] classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class F,RW,OLAP cold; ``` 4. **Flink** *(a stateful stream processor that runs tumbling 1-minute windows and counts events per key — it does the rollup so reads never touch raw data)* consumes each partition. 5. For each 1-minute tumbling window it emits `count per geohash` at the finest precision (p7) into **Redis** *(an in-memory key-value store — holds the current live grid for fast reads)*. 6. When a minute "seals," the aggregator also writes the finalized counts to the **columnar store** for history (F4). **Roll-up trick:** we only count at the finest precision (p7); coarser zoom levels (p6, p5, p4) are just **prefix sums** — a p6 cell's count is the sum of its child p7 cells. So one aggregation feeds every zoom level. > 💬 "Geohash has a lovely property: dropping a character zooms out one level. So I aggregate once at the finest grid and sum prefixes for coarser zooms — no separate pipeline per zoom level." ### 6.3 "Serve low-latency heatmap queries at multiple zoom levels" Add the read path: a query service that reads pre-aggregated cells from Redis, fronted by a CDN. ```mermaid flowchart TD V[Map viewers] -->|7 GET heatmap bbox precision| CDN[CDN edge cache] CDN -->|8 miss| QS[Query service] QS -->|9 read cells in bbox| RW[(Redis live aggregates)] RW -->|10 cells| QS --> CDN --> V classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class CDN,QS,RW hot; ``` 7. Viewer GETs the heatmap for a viewport+precision; (8) the **CDN** *(edge cache near the user)* serves most requests directly with a ~5–10s TTL. 9–10. On a miss, the query service computes which geohash cells fall in the bbox at that precision and does a single multi-key read from Redis, returning the grid. ### Final (high-level) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Write D[Drivers] -->|1 POST| IS[Ingest service] IS -->|2 produce| K[[Kafka by geohash]] K -->|3 consume| F[Flink 1-min windows] end subgraph ReadHot V[Viewers] -->|7 GET| CDN[CDN edge] CDN -->|miss| QS[Query service] QS --> RW[(Redis live grid
key geohash7 count)] end subgraph History OLAP[(Columnar store
minute,geohash,count)] S3[(Raw pings 7-30d)] end F -->|4 live counts| RW F -->|5 sealed minute| OLAP IS -.->|optional archive| S3 classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class CDN,QS,RW hot; class IS,K,F,OLAP,S3 cold; ``` > 🎙️ Script: "End to end: drivers POST pings, the ingest service shoves them into Kafka partitioned by city geohash, and Flink consumes those partitions running one-minute windows to count drivers per finest-grid cell. Those live counts go into Redis as the current grid, and each sealed minute also lands in a columnar store for history. On the read side, viewers hit a CDN that absorbs most traffic; misses go to a query service that just reads the cells in the viewport out of Redis. Coarser zoom levels are prefix sums of the fine grid, so one aggregation serves every zoom." 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton. The three places I can go deep are the ingestion firehose, the geo-aggregation and zoom roll-ups, or the hot-city scaling problem. Which is most interesting to you?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Fields | Store | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Location update | driverId, lat, lon, ts | Kafka (then S3 archive) | Append-only firehose; never queried by key | | Live cell aggregate | geohash7 → count, asOf | **Redis** (sharded by geohash prefix) | In-memory, ~100K read QPS, TTL auto-expires stale cells | | Historical aggregate | minute, geohash, precision, count | **Columnar OLAP** (ClickHouse/Druid) | Time-range scans, cheap compression | | Raw archive | partitioned parquet by hour/city | **S3** *(cheap object storage)* | Reprocessing / ML; deleted after 7–30d | - **Partition/shard key everywhere: geohash prefix.** Co-locates a city's data for both aggregation and reads, and a viewport query hits a bounded key range. - **Consistency:** Redis aggregates are eventually consistent with ~1-min lag; columnar store is the durable record of truth for history. - **TTL on Redis cells** (~2–3 min) so a cell that goes empty disappears instead of showing a stale ghost count. > 🎙️ Script: "Three stores for three jobs. Kafka and S3 hold the raw firehose nobody queries directly. Redis holds the live grid keyed by geohash for fast reads with a short TTL so empty cells vanish. And a columnar store like ClickHouse holds per-minute aggregates for years of cheap time-range analytics. Everything is partitioned by geohash prefix so a city's data stays together." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the firehose number (2.5M writes/s) and the 1-minute freshness SLO — every decision falls out of those two. ### How do we handle 2.5M location writes/s without melting a database? **#### Bad: write each ping straight to a geo-database** **Approach:** ingest service does an upsert per ping into Postgres/Mongo with a geo-index. **Challenges:** ~2.5M upserts/s is ~20x a single node; index maintenance per write is brutal; you'd shard into hundreds of nodes purely for write throughput you don't even need at read time. ⚠️ **Trap:** treating location pings as durable records — they're disposable; only the *aggregate* matters. **#### Good: batch into Redis counters directly** **Approach:** ingest increments a Redis counter per geohash cell. **Challenges:** still ~2.5M INCRs/s hammering hot city keys; no replay if Redis dies; counts drift because pings don't decrement when a driver leaves a cell — you'd need TTL gymnastics per driver. **#### Great: Kafka buffer + Flink windowed aggregation** **Approach:** pings → Kafka (partitioned by geohash prefix) → Flink tumbling 1-minute windows counting **distinct drivers per cell** → write the finished count to Redis. The window naturally handles "driver left the cell": each minute we recount from scratch, so counts self-heal — no decrement bookkeeping. ```mermaid flowchart TD K[[Kafka geohash partitions]] -->|consume| F[Flink tumbling 1-min] F -->|distinct driver count per geohash7| RW[(Redis grid)] F -->|sealed minute| OLAP[(Columnar store)] classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class F,RW,OLAP cold; ``` **Math:** 2.5M/s spread over ~250 Kafka partitions = ~10K msgs/s/partition — trivial. Flink parallelism scales with partitions. Redis only sees **one write per active cell per minute** — at ~200K active cells/city that's a few thousand writes/s, *four orders of magnitude* below the raw firehose. That collapse is the whole point. **Trade-off:** ~1-minute latency floor and Flink operational complexity — acceptable given the freshness SLO. > 🎙️ Script: "The trick is to never write pings durably. They go into Kafka, partitioned by city, and Flink counts distinct drivers per cell in one-minute windows. Because we recount each minute, a driver leaving a cell is handled for free — the count just doesn't include them next window. Redis then only gets one write per cell per minute, which turns a 2.5-million-a-second firehose into a few thousand writes a second." 🧠 If they ask "why not just sample pings?": "We can — dropping to 1 ping per driver per window cuts volume with zero accuracy loss for counts, since the window only needs to know a driver was present once. Kafka+Flink still gives us replay and history that pure sampling doesn't." ### How do we serve every zoom level without N pipelines? **#### Great: aggregate at finest precision, prefix-sum for coarser zooms** **Approach:** Geohash is hierarchical — `tdr1y` (p5) contains all `tdr1y*` (p6) cells. We count once at p7 and **roll up by truncating the geohash string**. A query at precision 5 truncates each p7 key to 5 chars and sums. **Trade-off:** summing millions of fine cells at query time for a zoomed-out city view is expensive. So we **pre-materialize** the common coarse levels (p4–p6) in Flink too — emit counts at p4,5,6,7 from the same window. Storage is tiny (coarser = far fewer cells), reads become a direct key lookup at any zoom. ```mermaid flowchart TD F[Flink window] -->|p7 fine count| RW[(Redis)] F -->|p6 p5 p4 rolled up| RW QS[Query service] -->|read precision N keys in bbox| RW classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class QS,RW hot; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class F cold; ``` > 🎙️ Script: "Geohash is hierarchical, so zooming out is literally chopping characters off the key. I pre-compute the counts at four precision levels in the same Flink window — coarse levels are tiny — so any zoom is a direct Redis read, no on-the-fly summing." 🧠 If they ask "why geohash over S2 or H3?": "All work; geohash wins here because its string-prefix property makes zoom roll-ups trivial. H3's hexagons avoid distortion near poles and look nicer, so for a polished product I'd consider H3 with its parent-cell API — same idea, hex instead of prefix." ### Hot-city / hot-cell problem (the binding bottleneck) **The bottleneck:** key distribution is uniform but **traffic isn't** — downtown Manhattan at rush hour has 100x the drivers and viewers of a suburb. One Redis shard owning that geohash prefix and one Kafka partition become hot. **Relief:** - **Kafka:** sub-partition hot prefixes by appending a salt (`geohash7 + driverId%N`) so a hot cell spreads across N partitions; Flink re-keys and merges. New ceiling: per-partition load bounded regardless of city density. - **Redis reads:** the CDN already absorbs ~95% of reads for popular downtown viewports (everyone looks at the same area). Residual misses hit a read replica per shard. - **Hot shard:** replicate the hot city's shard 3–5x for reads. **Math:** 100K read QPS at 95% CDN hit = ~5K QPS to Redis — one replica handles it. Cold cache (0% hit): 100K QPS needs ~2–3 replicas per shard. Manageable because the working set (visible viewports) is small. > 🎙️ Script: "The real danger isn't average load, it's downtown at rush hour. I salt hot geohash prefixes across multiple Kafka partitions so no single one melts, and on reads the CDN saves me — everyone's looking at the same downtown tile, so cache hit rates are high and Redis barely feels it." 🧠 If they ask "what if a single cell has a million drivers?": "Counts are cheap regardless of cardinality if I use a counter; if I want distinct-driver counts I'd use HyperLogLog *(a probabilistic counter that estimates uniques in ~12KB with ~2% error)* — perfect since heatmaps are approximate anyway." ### Idempotency & duplicate pings A driver's retry or at-least-once Kafka delivery can double-count. **Within a 1-minute window we count distinct driverId**, so a duplicate ping is naturally deduped — the same driver in the same cell counts once regardless of how many pings arrive. This makes the whole pipeline idempotent without a separate dedup store. A driver straddling two cells in one minute may be counted in both; for a heatmap that's acceptable noise. > 🎙️ Script: "Idempotency falls out of counting distinct drivers per window — duplicate pings collapse to one. I don't need a dedup store; the aggregation is dedup." ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost **Availability:** ingest 99.99% (Kafka replicated 3x, multi-AZ), read 99.9%. **Per-dependency degradation:** - **Redis down:** query service falls back to reading the *last sealed minute* from the columnar store — slower but the map still renders. Stale-but-up. - **Flink down:** Kafka retains data (replay on recovery); map freezes at last-good minute, no data lost. This is why the buffer matters. - **Kafka partition loss:** 3x replication + multi-AZ; consumers resume from committed offset. - **CDN down:** query service + replicas absorb full 100K QPS at higher cost. **RPO/RTO:** Raw pings RPO = a few seconds (Kafka), but they're disposable. Aggregates RPO ~1 min (last sealed window in OLAP), RTO ~minutes (Flink restart from offset). History (OLAP) is the durable record — replicated, backed up; tested restore monthly. **Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is **Kafka + Flink compute** for the firehose (~2.5M/s sustained) and CDN egress for reads, not storage — aggregates are only a few TB. Raw archive in S3 is cheap because we expire it in days. Ballpark mid five figures/month for a large multi-city deployment; I'd cut it by sampling pings (1 per driver per window) — the single biggest lever. **Observability:** SLO burn-rate alerts on (a) freshness lag = now − last sealed minute, (b) Kafka consumer lag, (c) read p99; distributed tracing on the read path; runbook for "map is stale" → check consumer lag first. > 🎙️ Script: "The buffer is also my reliability story. If Flink or Redis dies, Kafka holds everything and the map just freezes at the last good minute instead of losing data — and if Redis is gone, reads fall back to the last sealed minute in the columnar store. Nothing here is a single point of total failure, and the dominant cost is the streaming compute, which I'd attack by sampling pings." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger - **1-minute aggregation latency (least sure):** gives huge throughput collapse and self-healing counts, but the map lags reality by up to a minute. If the SLO became sub-second, I'd switch to sliding windows + incremental counters and pay in complexity and cost. - **Approximate counts / sampling:** we accept noise to save compute. Reverses if a use case (e.g. driver pay, compliance) needs exact counts — then we drop sampling and add exactly-once Flink checkpointing. - **Geohash over H3:** prefix roll-ups are simplest, but rectangular cells distort and look blocky. At product-polish scale I'd move to H3 hexagons; reverses on UX priority, not throughput. - **Pre-materializing 4 zoom levels:** trades a little write/storage for O(1) reads at any zoom. Reverses only if we needed dozens of arbitrary precision levels — then compute-on-read with caching. > 🎙️ Script: "My least-certain call is the one-minute window — it's the source of all the efficiency but also the staleness. If they need real-time-real-time, that's the first thing I'd revisit, trading simplicity for sliding windows." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **Q1. Why Kafka and not just a load balancer to aggregators?** Kafka gives durable buffering, replay, and decoupling: if aggregators fall behind or crash, no pings are lost and they resume from offset. A direct LB-to-aggregator path drops data the moment a consumer hiccups. It also lets us partition by geohash so each city's data is co-located. The cost is operational complexity, which is worth it at 2.5M/s. > 💬 "Kafka is my shock absorber — it decouples a firehose I can't slow down from consumers that occasionally stall." **Q2. How do counts decrease when drivers leave an area?** We don't decrement — each 1-minute tumbling window recounts from scratch, so a driver who left simply isn't present in the next window's count. This self-healing avoids fragile per-driver decrement bookkeeping. The cost is up to a minute of staleness, which matches our SLO. > 💬 "I never decrement. Every minute I recount, so leaving a cell is handled for free." **Q3. How do you serve different zoom levels efficiently?** Geohash is hierarchical, so I aggregate at the finest precision and pre-materialize coarser levels by truncating the key in the same Flink window. Any zoom becomes a direct Redis lookup of the right-precision keys in the viewport. No query-time summation of millions of cells. > 💬 "Zooming out is just chopping characters off a geohash, and I pre-compute those levels so every zoom is a direct read." **Q4. What happens at peak in a dense downtown — hot shards?** That's the binding bottleneck: traffic is non-uniform even if keys are. I salt hot geohash prefixes across multiple Kafka partitions and re-merge in Flink, and on reads the CDN absorbs most of the duplicate downtown viewport requests. The hot city's Redis shard gets extra read replicas. > 💬 "Downtown rush hour is the real test — I salt hot keys across partitions and lean on the CDN since everyone's viewing the same tile." **Q5. How do you avoid double-counting from retries / at-least-once delivery?** The window counts distinct driverIds, so duplicate pings collapse to one — the aggregation is inherently idempotent. No separate dedup store needed. A driver straddling a cell boundary may appear in two cells, which is acceptable heatmap noise. > 💬 "Counting distinct drivers per window means duplicates just disappear — dedup is free." **Q6. How is history stored and queried?** Each sealed minute's aggregates go to a columnar store like ClickHouse, partitioned by time and geohash, giving cheap compressed time-range scans for trend analysis. Raw pings go to S3 as parquet but expire in days since they're a petabyte/year. Analysts query the OLAP store, never the live cache. > 💬 "Per-minute aggregates live for years in a columnar store; raw pings live for days in S3 for reprocessing." **Q7. Exactly-once vs at-least-once?** Default is at-least-once with idempotent distinct-counting, which is cheaper and sufficient for approximate heatmaps. If a downstream consumer needed exactness (billing, compliance), I'd enable Flink's checkpointed exactly-once with transactional Kafka writes, paying in latency and throughput. I'd scope exactly-once only to that consumer. > 💬 "At-least-once is fine because the count is idempotent; I'd only pay for exactly-once where money depends on it." **Q8. How fresh is the map, really, and can you make it faster?** Up to ~1 minute behind, set by the tumbling window. To get to seconds I'd use overlapping sliding windows or incremental counters that emit every few seconds, at higher compute cost and more complex state. I'd only do it if a product need justified it. > 💬 "It's about a minute fresh; sub-second is possible with sliding windows but I wouldn't pay for it unless asked." **Q9. How do you cap the read load — 100K QPS is a lot?** Most viewers look at the same popular viewports, so a CDN with a 5–10s TTL gives ~95% hit rates, leaving only a few thousand QPS to Redis replicas. The TTL also bounds staleness to the same order as our freshness SLO, so caching costs us nothing in correctness. Cold-cache worst case needs 2–3 replicas per shard. > 💬 "The CDN does the heavy lifting — everyone looks at downtown, so hit rates are high and Redis barely sweats." **Q10. How would you extend this to show driver *availability* heat, not just density?** Each ping carries a status (available/on-trip); Flink keys the window by (geohash, status) and emits separate counts, or maintains a ratio per cell. Redis stores both layers, and the read API takes a `layer` param. Same pipeline, richer payload — no architectural change, which is the point of aggregating generically. > 💬 "I'd just add status to the window key and emit per-status counts — the pipeline doesn't change, the payload gets richer." **Q11. What if you need this globally, multi-region?** Cities are independent, so I run the pipeline per region and route by geohash — a viewport query is always region-local, so no cross-region reads on the hot path. History replicates async to a central OLAP for global analytics. Single-write-region per city avoids multi-master conflicts entirely. > 💬 "Geo-partitioning makes multi-region easy — each city is its own pipeline and queries are always local." **Q12. How do you handle bad/spoofed GPS data?** Validate lat/lon ranges and plausibility (speed jumps) at ingest, and since counts are approximate, a few bad points barely move a cell. For systematic spoofing I'd add anomaly detection on driver trajectories downstream, but it's out of the heatmap's critical path. The aggregation's averaging nature is itself robust to noise. > 💬 "Heatmaps are averages, so noise washes out; I do basic plausibility checks at ingest and leave fraud detection to a separate consumer." 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "We're building a real-time driver density heatmap. The defining fact is asymmetry — about two and a half million location pings a second coming in, versus a hundred thousand reads that all want the same pre-computed grid. So pings never touch a database: they go into Kafka partitioned by city geohash, and Flink counts distinct drivers per cell in one-minute windows. That collapses the firehose into a few thousand Redis writes a second, and because we recount each minute, drivers leaving a cell self-heal with no bookkeeping. We aggregate at the finest geohash precision and pre-materialize coarser zoom levels by truncating the key, so any zoom is a direct lookup. Reads go through a CDN that absorbs most traffic since everyone views the same downtown tiles. Sealed minutes land in a columnar store for years of cheap history; raw pings expire in days. The whole thing is intentionally approximate and eventually consistent — that single relaxation is what makes the scale tractable. The real risks I'd watch are hot downtown shards, which I handle with key-salting and CDN caching, and the one-minute freshness floor, which I'd only tighten with sliding windows if the product truly needed it."
